title: Whittled lime leaf pendant
subtitle: A carved leaf-form pendant in seasoned lime with a drilled hanging hole
excerpt: A lime wood pendant carved in the form of a simple leaf. Seasoned lime holds fine detail at thin sections. The project introduces relief shaping and shows how the sloyd knife handles cross-grain cuts in a small format.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the small-relief carving method; Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry for the whittling-grain conventions.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried wood at a stable moisture content of 8 to 12 percent for indoor use.
The rough-shaped piece of wood from which the finished item is carved.
A short vertical cut into the wood that stops the next push or pull cut from tearing the grain past the intended end-point.
Three or more cuts converging to lift a small triangular chip from the surface. The chip-carving fundamental.

content.text: A carved leaf pendant shows what a sloyd knife can do with
seasoned

lime in a small format: clean cross-grain cuts, a raised central rib, and a tapered edge that reads as the leaf margin. Lime is the sculptor's standard because it holds fine detail without splitting under light cuts and sands evenly. The pendant measures 50 mm long and 30 mm wide and weighs almost nothing when finished.
Wood
Lime (basswood) is the recommendation: fine grain, carves cleanly across the grain with very light pressure, and holds the thin edge of the leaf margin without splitting. The
blank

needs to be 55 mm long, 35 mm wide, and 8 mm thick. Cut it from a piece of dried lime board or carving block. Birch is an alternative: slightly coarser grain, still holds the edge well. Cherry gives a warm colour when oiled but is harder to cut at thin sections.
Tools
Sloyd knife: for all shaping and the central rib detail.
Cordless drill, 18V with a 3 mm bit: for the hanging hole at the leaf stem.
Leather strop: strop every fifteen minutes.
Sandpaper, 320 grit (optional): for final surface refinement before oiling.
Danish oil for finishing: builds a soft satin that brings out the pale lime grain.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
Cutting the outline

Draw the leaf shape on one face of the blank: a symmetrical simple leaf with a pointed tip and a short stem. The widest point is 30 mm, about one-third down from the tip. Use the sloyd knife to cut the outline from the edge of the blank inward. This is a cross-grain cut on the sides: set a
stop cut
first at the widest point, then pare down to it from above and below. Without the stop cut, the cross-grain cut will run along the grain and past the leaf margin.
The central rib
The central rib runs from the stem to the tip along the top face of the pendant. Mark it with a pencil line. Use
chip cuts

to lower the leaf face on each side of the rib by 1.5 mm, leaving the rib proud. Each chip cut starts 3 mm from the rib centreline with a slicing cut angled toward the rib, and a second cut from the far side of the chip angled back. The chip lifts cleanly. Work three or four chips per side along the length of the rib; the finished rib is 2 mm wide.
Thinning the leaf margin

The leaf edge should taper from 6 mm thick at the rib to 1.5 mm at the very edge. Hold the pendant between two fingers of the non-knife hand and use light push cuts angled outward toward the edge. Work slowly; at 1.5 mm thickness lime is fragile. Stop before the edge looks translucent in direct light; a translucent section means less than 1 mm of wood and it will crack with wear.
The hanging hole and stem

The stem is 8 mm long and 5 mm wide. Drill the 3 mm hanging hole through the stem with the cordless drill before the stem is too narrow to hold without breaking. Clamp the pendant in a folded cloth to keep it steady while drilling. Smooth the drill entry and exit with the knife tip.
Finishing

Sand lightly with 320-grit if a very smooth surface is wanted. Apply one thin coat of Danish oil with a fingertip; wipe off after ten minutes. Allow to dry for four hours. The Danish oil builds a soft satin in one coat on a small piece this size. Lime will remain pale; the oil adds very little colour.
Variations
Feather form: elongate the blank to 70 mm and add lateral chip-cuts along both sides of the central rib for a quill-feather effect.
Pyrography veins: add veining lines to the leaf surface with a fine pyrography tip before oiling.
Cherry for warmth: cherry takes the Danish oil to a deeper amber that suits autumn-leaf colouring.
Care
Keep the pendant away from prolonged water contact. A very light re-oil every year or two is enough to keep the surface alive.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Stop thinning before the tip looks translucent. A snapped tip cannot be reattached; re-carve the leaf shape slightly shorter to remove the break point.

attrs.symptom: The rib is crooked when viewed along the length
attrs.cause: Chip cuts worked unevenly from one side
attrs.fix: Work equal numbers of chip cuts on both sides of the rib in alternating passes, not all one side first. Check alignment by holding the pendant at eye level along its length.

attrs.symptom: The edge split rather than cut on the leaf margin
attrs.cause: Cross-grain cut without a stop cut
attrs.fix: Set the stop cut first at every change of direction along the leaf margin. Lime's fine grain makes this especially important; without the stop cut the split travels invisibly along the grain ahead of the blade.
